# Causeway Bay: Insiders’ Favourites

Causeway Bay Cultural Exploration Food & Drink Shopping Wan Chai [...] Causeway bay is known as Hong Kong’s centre of everything cool and hip. Here you can find everything from hidden designer stores to trendy bars, and the newest gourmet experiences.

Here are some of our top picks.

WhatsApp

Facebook

X

E-mail

Copy Link

Copied successfully

Sijie

Sijie

Part private kitchen, part restaurant, what Sijie lacks in pomp and circumstance it more than makes up for in authenticity, flavour and value for money. Sharing dishes is the order of the day, so it is better to go as a large group. Be certain to sample the Sichuan cold noodles — deliciously chewy noodles in mouth-numbing fiery chilli oil — and the poached fish in hot chilli oil. Book ahead as Sijie only has eight tables.

See More...

SKYE

SKYE [...] See More...

Plumcot

Plumcot

Finding a good patisserie in Hong Kong is not always easy. At least, that is, until Tai Hang’s Plumcot opened its doors in summer 2017. Run by a husband and wife team who knead, fold and glaze everything themselves, the bijou bakery has earned a reputation as one of the best in town, not only for its perfectly flaky croissants but also for its experimental ice creams, including the intriguing blue cheese flavour. Arrive early for a piece of the pie, it sells out on an almost daily basis.

See More...

Ink Clothing

Ink Clothing

# Causeway Bay: Insiders’ Favourites

[Causeway Bay](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:location/hong-kong-island/wan-chai/causeway-bay) [Cultural Exploration](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:purpose/cultural-exploration) [Food & Drink](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/eat-and-drink) [Shopping](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/shopping) [Wan Chai](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:location/hong-kong-island/wan-chai)

Causeway bay is known as Hong Kong’s centre of everything cool and hip. Here you can find everything from hidden designer stores to trendy bars, and the newest gourmet experiences.

Here are some of our top picks.

WhatsApp

Facebook

X

E-mail

Copy Link

Copied successfully

![Sijie]()

Sijie

Part private kitchen, part restaurant, what [Sijie](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-sijie.html) lacks in pomp and circumstance it more than makes up for in authenticity, flavour and value for money. Sharing dishes is the order of the day, so it is better to go as a large group. Be certain to sample the Sichuan cold noodles — deliciously chewy noodles in mouth-numbing fiery chilli oil — and the poached fish in hot chilli oil. Book ahead as Sijie only has eight tables.

See More...

![SKYE]()

SKYE

In a city as vertically inclined as Hong Kong, head upward to [Skye](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-skye.html) , perched atop The Park Lane Hong Kong to enjoy a drink with a view. A space-age vision of sweeping curves and colour-changing illuminations, it offers both indoor and al fresco seating. To take full advantage of the spectacular Victoria Harbour vista, settle outside, where the twinkling cityscape provides a picture-perfect backdrop against which to enjoy one of the bar’s playfully named cocktails, all set to the soundtrack of an in-house DJ.

See More...

![Plumcot]()

Plumcot

Finding a good patisserie in Hong Kong is not always easy. At least, that is, until Tai Hang’s [Plumcot](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-plumcot.html) opened its doors in summer 2017. Run by a husband and wife team who knead, fold and glaze everything themselves, the bijou bakery has earned a reputation as one of the best in town, not only for its perfectly flaky croissants but also for its experimental ice creams, including the intriguing blue cheese flavour. Arrive early for a piece of the pie, it sells out on an almost daily basis.

See More...

![Ink Clothing]()

Ink Clothing

Favoured by Causeway Bay’s monochrome-clad denizens, [Ink](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-ink-clothing.html) is renowned among the city’s style set for its curation of menswear, womenswear and accessories from pioneering European designers, all artfully arranged within minimal surrounds. The store shuns mainstream labels, instead you will find little-known, next-big-thing brands such as Delada and Carol Christian Poell hanging out alongside the likes of DRKSHDW for Rick Owens and Ambust. Better still, the staff are welcoming, helpful and lack pretension, which makes Ink’s high-fashion credibility all the more legitimate.

See More...

Information on this page is subject to change without advance notice.

results.title: E-guidebooks | Hong Kong Tourism Board
results.content:
...See more

Download

;)

#### Lei Yue Mun

Follow this guidebook while strolling along the Lei Yue Mun waterfront promenade to explore the area’s heritage and cultural attractions and uncover the stories behind them.

...See more

Download

;)

#### Sham Shui Po

Sham Shui Po is one of Hong Kong’s most authentic and distinctive neighbourhoods. Here, traditional nostalgic shops selling sewing supplies, toys and electrical appliances stand alongside trendy cafes and artisanal boutiques, blending the old with the new. Follow this guidebook to stroll through Sham Shui Po to get a genuine taste of the neighbourhood’s distinctive character.

...See more

Download

;)

#### Tai Hang [...] #### Explore Hong Kong’s Halal Flavours

A handy food guide for Muslim travellers in Hong Kong

...See more

Download

;)

#### Hong Kong Map

Download ‘Hong Kong Map’ to discover key attractions in different districts and get tips for a smooth trip!

...See more

Download

;)

#### Central

Central has witnessed Hong Kong’s transformation from a humble fishing village to an international metropolis. Follow this guidebook to unveil discoveries around every corner, tracing Central’s remarkable journey through the past and present.

...See more

Download

;)

#### Kowloon City

Kowloon City is a district in Hong Kong with a rich heritage that has always been closely connected to the development of the city. Follow this guidebook to explore this featured community.

...See more

Download [...] ...See more

Download

;)

#### Tai Hang

Adjacent to the bustling streets of Causeway Bay, Tai Hang preserves its rich historical and cultural heritage alongside the scent of the local community. Follow this guidebook to trace the path of the fire dragon and embark on a journey through time, exploring the stories hidden within every corner of Tai Hang.

...See more

Download

;)

#### The Peak

Ascend to the top of Victoria Peak, and the skyline of Victoria Harbour comes into full view. The Peak offers the most beautiful vantage point to admire Hong Kong’s beauty and is a must-visit attraction for visitors. Follow this guidebook to explore the Peak’s historical architecture and attractions, along with stories of the Peak and Victoria Harbour.

...See more

Download

;)

## **E-guidebooks**

If you’re trying to get an overview of Hong Kong, you’ve come to the right place. Download e-guidebooks to discover different facets of Hong Kong.

[![Explore Hong Kong’s Halal Flavours](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/muslim-travel/explore-hong-kong-halal-flavours-960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Explore Hong Kong’s Halal Flavours

A handy food guide for Muslim travellers in Hong Kong

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/muslim-travel/explore-hong-kong-halal-flavours.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/hong-kong-map-960x720-entc.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Hong Kong Map

Download ‘Hong Kong Map’ to discover key attractions in different districts and get tips for a smooth trip!

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/hong-kong-map.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/central_960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Central

Central has witnessed Hong Kong’s transformation from a humble fishing village to an international metropolis. Follow this guidebook to unveil discoveries around every corner, tracing Central’s remarkable journey through the past and present.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/cityintime-ebook-central-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/kowloon-city-960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Kowloon City

Kowloon City is a district in Hong Kong with a rich heritage that has always been closely connected to the development of the city. Follow this guidebook to explore this featured community.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/kowloon-city-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/neighbourhoods/lei-yue-mun-fishing-village/lei-yue-mun-fishing-villag-960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Lei Yue Mun

Follow this guidebook while strolling along the Lei Yue Mun waterfront promenade to explore the area’s heritage and cultural attractions and uncover the stories behind them.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/lei-yue-mun-ebook-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/sham-shui-po-960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Sham Shui Po

Sham Shui Po is one of Hong Kong’s most authentic and distinctive neighbourhoods. Here, traditional nostalgic shops selling sewing supplies, toys and electrical appliances stand alongside trendy cafes and artisanal boutiques, blending the old with the new. Follow this guidebook to stroll through Sham Shui Po to get a genuine taste of the neighbourhood’s distinctive character.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/sham-shui-po-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/tai_hang_960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Tai Hang

Adjacent to the bustling streets of Causeway Bay, Tai Hang preserves its rich historical and cultural heritage alongside the scent of the local community. Follow this guidebook to trace the path of the fire dragon and embark on a journey through time, exploring the stories hidden within every corner of Tai Hang.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/cityintime-ebook-tai_hang-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/the-peak-960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### The Peak

Ascend to the top of Victoria Peak, and the skyline of Victoria Harbour comes into full view. The Peak offers the most beautiful vantage point to admire Hong Kong’s beauty and is a must-visit attraction for visitors. Follow this guidebook to explore the Peak’s historical architecture and attractions, along with stories of the Peak and Victoria Harbour.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/the-peak-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/tsim-sha-tsui960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Tsim Sha Tsui

Tsim Sha Tsui is one of Hong Kong’s iconic international districts and has long been a vibrant hub where East meets West. Centuries-old historical buildings stand in striking contrast to modern skyscrapers, creating a unique urban landscape. Follow this guidebook and stroll through Tsim Sha Tsui to uncover the community’s past across multiple eras.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/tsim-sha-tsui-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/yau-ma-tei-960x720.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### Yau Ma Tei

Yau Ma Tei carries a century of Hong Kong’s commercial and popular cultural memories, blending tradition with modernity, creating a neighbourhood that is both unique and distinctive. Follow this guidebook to stroll through Yau Ma Tei and explore this warm and vibrant community in depth.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/yau-ma-tei-en.pdf)

[![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/shataukok-guidebook-557x395-en.jpg)](javascript:void(0);)

#### A tourist’s guide to Sha Tau Kok

Discover historic and authentic treasures in Sha Tau Kok, a Frontier Closed Area in Hong Kong that will gradually open for tourism activities after implementation of the Government’s Northern Metropolis Development Strategy.

...See more

[Download](/content/dam/dhk/intl/plan/traveller-info/e-guidebooks/shataukok-guidebook-en.pdf)

# Yummy Halal Food Spots Around Favourite City Locations

![]()

Many are concerned about whether it’s possible to find local halal food in Hong Kong, and some even consider taking instant noodles with them. But there’s really no need for that. You’ll be happy to know that Hong Kong is actually full of yummy halal food (including authentic local food), with much of it found near major attractions! These are just a few of our favourite spots in different areas.

### Along Victoria Harbour and Kowloon City

Hong Kong’s breathtaking skyline is best witnessed at [Victoria Harbour](/eng/attractions/best-ways-to-marvel-at-iconic-victoria-harbour.html). After grabbing dinner at the yummy eateries nearby, head to the waterfront to witness the gorgeous lights and music of ‘[A Symphony of Lights](/eng/attractions/choreographing-the-symphony-of-lights.html)’ — a multimedia show that is recognised as one of the world’s most spectacular! If you’re looking for a different perspective of Hong Kong, be sure to take a spin on the Hong Kong Observation Wheel.

![Victoria Harbour]()

#### Happy Cow

The Happy Cow dessert kiosk serving vegan, milkshakes and ice cream is conveniently located right next to the Observation Wheel. Choose from a wide variety of flavours — from Ying Yang Sesame (a signature local flavour) to Pina Colada (alcohol-free).

![Happy Cow]()

Enjoy your ice cream on the lawn in front of the shop, as you take in the gorgeous view of Hong Kong around you — the perfect way to relax and unwind.

Address: Booth 3, Man Kwong Street, Hong Kong Observation Wheel, Central, Hong Kong Island

### In Causeway Bay

![Causeway Bay]()

Hong Kong’s major shopping district consists not only of popular international brands but also many halal food places, perfect for keeping your tummies full while you shop.

#### Wai Kee

A short 7-minute walk from Causeway Bay Station lies a renowned halal roast duck stall. Wai Kee might not be the only stall where you can find halal roast duck, but it’s known as one of the best in Hong Kong, period.

![Wai Kee]()

And you’ll understand why once you sink your teeth into the tender duck meat. The flavour coupled with the golden brown, crispy skin is indescribable. But don’t just come here for the duck rice! The wonton noodles, soy chicken rice and signature mutton curry rice are firm favourites too.

![Bowrington Road Cooked Food Centre]()

Halal status: Muslim-owned  
Address:  Shop 5, Bowrington Road Cooked Food Centre, 1/F, Bowrington Road Market, 21 Bowrington Road, Wan Chai, Hong Kong Island

#### Islamic Centre Canteen

Islamic Centre Canteen is a convenient 10-minute walk from MTR Causeway Bay Station and is quite easy to find, as it’s located in a mosque.

![Islamic Centre Canteen]()

Choose from their range of steamed and fried *dim sum*. Our top picks for steamed would be the *siu mai* made with chicken, which is soft and succulent, as well as *har gow* (steamed shrimp dumplings). For the fried, we particularly recommend the deep-fried shrimp wonton.

![ ]( " ")

##### 

If you’re coming here only for the *dim sum*, remember to do so before 3pm, as the *dim sum* menu is not available after!

If you’re in Causeway Bay close to dinner time, try Islamic Centre Canteen’s dinner menu. For a place known for their *dim sum*, we think you’ll be pleasantly surprised at how good their Cantonese main dishes are.

Halal status: halal-certified  
Address: 5th floor, Osman Ramju Sadick, 40 Oi Kwan Road, Wan Chai, Hong Kong Island

### Attractions

#### Ocean Park

![Main Street Market]()

This might be [Hong Kong’s oldest theme park](/eng/attractions/the-ultimate-guide-to-ocean-park-hong-kong.html) but its age is only testament to how awesome it is! There’s so much to do and see that you’ll have to spend at least a full day here. And we know just what you need to fuel all that running around — food!

#### Panda Cafè Food to Go

Located at the Waterfront, this is a take-away kiosk offering a wide variety of food! Choose from their selection of meals or, if you prefer something lighter, opt for one of the small bites such as *baus* (buns) and hotdogs. Although it’s take away, you can sit and enjoy your meal in the cafeteria next door (not halal-certified).

![Panda Food To Go]()

The Hainan chicken rice and beef ball steamed rice make a great lunch. The portions are huge and will definitely sustain you for the day.️ For a healthier option, choose their salad bowl.

Halal status: halal-certified

If you’re already wondering how to fit all this deliciousness into your schedule, just wait until you check out [the full list of certified halal food outlets](/eng/themed-travel/muslim-travel.html#dining) in Hong Kong. You might just need to make a second trip!

Content provided by [Have Halal Will Travel](https://www.havehalalwilltravel.com/category/travel-guide/hong-kong-travel-guide). Information in this article is subject to change without advance notice. Please contact the relevant product or service providers for enquiries.

results.title: 7 Must-Visit Cha Chaan Tengs in Hong Kong | Hong Kong Tourism Board
results.content:
Cha chaan tengs, or 'tea restaurants,' embody Hong Kong’s unique East-meets-West food culture. With smooth milk tea, buttery pineapple buns, and flaky egg tarts, these comfort foods offer a delightful fusion.

Originating in the 1950s as bing sutts, or 'ice rooms,' they provided cool drinks for workers under ceiling fans. Initially serving simple dishes, these spots evolved into full diners. Today, cha chaan tengs  are a must-visit for an authentic taste of Hong Kong!

# 7 Must-Visit Cha Chaan Tengs in Hong Kong

[Budget Friendly](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:purpose/budget-friendly) [Cha Chaan Teng](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/eat-and-drink/local-flavours/cha-chaan-teng) [First-Timer](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:persona/first-timer) [Food & Drink](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/eat-and-drink) [Foodie](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:persona/foodie) [Local Flavours](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/eat-and-drink/local-flavours)

*Cha chaan tengs,* or 'tea restaurants,' embody Hong Kong’s unique East-meets-West food culture. With smooth milk tea, buttery pineapple buns, and flaky egg tarts, these comfort foods offer a delightful fusion.

Originating in the 1950s as *bing sutts,* or 'ice rooms,' they provided cool drinks for workers under ceiling fans. Initially serving simple dishes, these spots evolved into full diners. Today, *cha chaan tengs*  are a must-visit for an authentic taste of Hong Kong!

![Lan Fong Yuen in Central]()

Lan Fong Yuen, Central

***Classic dai pai dong***

See More...

Tucked away on Gage Street in Central, [Lan Fong Yuen](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-lan-fong-yuen.html) is easy to miss despite its iconic status. This small eatery started as a humble *dai pai dong* (outdoor food stall), before expanding its indoor diner. The classic shack still stands, serving both as a photo opportunity and kitchen where you can see its legendary ‘silk stocking milk tea’ being brewed and served. Peek inside and see how they strain the tea through a sackcloth bag that resembles a pair of silk stockings, before adding condensed milk and serving it either hot or over ice.

**Must-Try**: The traditional ‘silk stocking milk tea’ is as silky as it sounds.

See More...

![Mido Cafe in Yau Ma Tei]()

Mido Cafe, Yau Ma Tei

***Vintage diner that captures old Hong Kong***

See More...

[Mido Cafe](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-mido-cafe.html) is a timeless gem in Hong Kong’s *cha chaan teng*  scene, serving up nostalgia and classic dishes since the 1950s. Stepping inside feels like travelling back in time, with its original stained-glass windows, wooden booths and retro wall tiles. The menu offers comforting favourites like egg sandwiches, hearty noodle bowls and their signature milk tea, brewed to perfection. Mido Cafe has also made a name for itself in Hong Kong cinema, appearing in iconic films like *The World of Suzie Wong* and *Moonlight Express*.

**Must-Try**: A plate of the thickly-sliced Hong Kong-style French toast which comes dripping indulgently with butter and condensed milk.

See More...

![The classic pineapple bun with butter and milk tea at Kam Wah Cafe in Hong Kong.]()

Kam Wah Cafe, Prince Edward

***The best pineapple bun in Hong Kong?***

See More...

You’ll have to stop by [Kam Wah Cafe](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-kam-wah-cafe.html) and find out for yourself! Located in bustling Mong Kok, this family-owned cafe has been serving up classic Hong Kong comfort food since 1973. People from near and far flock here for its legendary pineapple bun — light, fluffy bread with a perfectly crispy, sugary top, often paired with a thick slab of butter. It’s a mouthwatering treat that melts in your mouth. Pair it with their smooth milk tea, and you’ve got the perfect snack. Alongside the famous buns, Kam Wah offers other *cha chaan teng* favourites like French toast, scrambled eggs and egg tarts.

**Must-Try**: The classic pineapple bun with butter.

See More...

![Scrambled egg sandwich served at a cha chaan teng.]()

Australian Dairy Company, Jordan

***Famously no-nonsense service***

See More...

When it comes to the best scrambled eggs in Hong Kong, the [Australian Dairy Company](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-australia-dairy-company.html) is a top contender. This iconic *cha chaan teng*  has been serving locals and visitors for over 50 years. Named after its founder’s time spent working on Australian farms, the cafe is known for using premium Aussie dairy in its signature dishes. The star of the show is the perfectly fluffy, creamy scrambled eggs with thick, buttered toast. If you’ve got room for more, the steamed milk and custard desserts are must-trys. The queues are long and the pace is rushed but the delicious food makes every moment worthwhile.

**Must-Try**: For a satisfying breakfast, order fluffy scrambled eggs and thick cut toast, with macaroni on the side.

See More...

![Cheung Hing Coffee Shop in Happy Valley]()

Cheung Hing Coffee Shop, Happy Valley

***Step back in time***

See More...

Cheung Hing Coffee Shop in Happy Valley is a charming throwback to 1950s Hong Kong. This rustic 70-year-old *cha chaan teng* features wooden booths, classic floor tiles and drinks served in traditional milkshake glasses, adding to its timeless appeal. Known for its famous pineapple buns and egg tarts, Cheung Hing also serves all the staples such as fried noodles with luncheon meat, scrambled eggs and Hong Kong-style milk tea.

**Must-Try**: The egg tarts! Sweet, flaky and perfectly balanced, they’re a hit among all who visit.

See More...

![A sandwich served in Waso Cafe]()

Waso Cafe, various locations

***A family-run cha chaan teng***

See More...

[Waso Cafe](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-mrs-tang-cafe.html), also known as Waso Cafe, started as a popular stop along the [Ping Shan Heritage Trail](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-ping-shan-heritage-trail.html) in Yuen Long and now has branches across Hong Kong. Known for its mouthwatering range of pineapple buns, hearty sandwiches and comforting noodle dishes, Waso Cafe offers something for everyone. Whether you're craving a classic pineapple bun, a filling sandwich or a simple bowl of noodles, this cosy spot has you covered.

**Must-Try**: The pork chop pineapple bun or the corned beef, cheese and egg toast.

See More...

![Tai Hing is a local chain of Hong Kong cha chaan tengs]()

Tai Hing, various locations

***A 30-year-old local chain***

See More...

Tai Hing has become a household name in Hong Kong, with locations all across the city. Their traditional barbeque pork has been popular since the beginning, and now they also serve up quick, easy and delicious *cha chaan teng* comfort food.

**Must-Try**: The *siu mei,* also known as Hong Kong-style roast meat.

See More...

results.title: Kowloon - Wikipedia
results.content:
## Administration

[edit]

Main articles: Kowloon Central "Kowloon Central (disambiguation)"), Kowloon East, and Kowloon West

Kowloon comprises the following districts:

 Kowloon City
 Kwun Tong
 Sham Shui Po
 Wong Tai Sin
 Yau Tsim Mong

## Name

[edit]

The name Kowloon (Chinese: 九龍 lit. "Nine Dragons") alludes to eight mountains and a Chinese emperor: Kowloon Peak, Tung Shan "Tung Shan (mountain)"), Tate's Cairn, Temple Hill "Temple Hill (Hong Kong)"), Unicorn Ridge, Lion Rock, Beacon Hill "Beacon Hill (Hong Kong)"), Crow's Nest "Crow's Nest (Hong Kong)") and Emperor Bing of Song. It has been variously transcribed and translated over the years as Kaulung or Kawloong, among others .

## History

"Kowloon" is a transliteration of 九龍. For other transliterations, see [九龍 (disambiguation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/九龍_(disambiguation) "九龍 (disambiguation)").

**Kowloon** ([/ˌkaʊˈluːn/](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")), [formerly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Government_Cantonese_Romanization "Hong Kong Government Cantonese Romanization") **Kaulung**, is one of the [three areas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Areas_of_Hong_Kong "Areas of Hong Kong") of [Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), along with [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") and the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"). It is an urban area comprising the [Kowloon Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Peninsula "Kowloon Peninsula") and [New Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Kowloon "New Kowloon"). It has a population of 2,019,533 and a population density of 43,033/km2 (111,450/sq mi) in 2006. It is the smallest, second most populous, and most densely populated of the divisions.

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Location")]

Kowloon is located directly north of [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") across [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour"). It is bordered by the [Lei Yue Mun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lei_Yue_Mun "Lei Yue Mun") strait to the east, [Mei Foo Sun Chuen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mei_Foo_Sun_Chuen "Mei Foo Sun Chuen"), [Butterfly Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butterfly_Valley "Butterfly Valley"), and [Stonecutter's Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stonecutters_Island "Stonecutters Island") to the west, a mountain range, including [Tate's Cairn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tate's_Cairn "Tate's Cairn") and [Lion Rock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lion_Rock "Lion Rock") to the north and [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") to the south.

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Name")]

The name **Kowloon** ([Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traditional_Chinese "Traditional Chinese"): 九龍 lit. "Nine Dragons") alludes to eight mountains and a Chinese emperor: [Kowloon Peak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Peak "Kowloon Peak"), [Tung Shan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tung_Shan_(mountain) "Tung Shan (mountain)"), [Tate's Cairn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tate's_Cairn "Tate's Cairn"), [Temple Hill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temple_Hill_(Hong_Kong) "Temple Hill (Hong Kong)"), [Unicorn Ridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unicorn_Ridge "Unicorn Ridge"), [Lion Rock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lion_Rock "Lion Rock"), [Beacon Hill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beacon_Hill_(Hong_Kong) "Beacon Hill (Hong Kong)"), [Crow's Nest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crow's_Nest_(Hong_Kong) "Crow's Nest (Hong Kong)") and [Emperor Bing of Song](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emperor_Bing_of_Song "Emperor Bing of Song").[[1]](#cite_note-Fallon-1) It has been variously transcribed and translated over the years as **Kaulung** or **Kawloong**, among others .[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Kowloon_City%2C_Mainland%2C_opposite_Hong_Kong.jpg/250px-Kowloon_City%2C_Mainland%2C_opposite_Hong_Kong.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kowloon_City,_Mainland,_opposite_Hong_Kong.jpg)

Kowloon c. 1868, depicting the [Qing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qing_empire "Qing empire")-era [Kowloon Walled City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Walled_City "Kowloon Walled City") and [Lion Rock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lion_Rock "Lion Rock") (in the background)

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Victoria_City_and_Kowloon_1915.jpg/250px-Victoria_City_and_Kowloon_1915.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Victoria_City_and_Kowloon_1915.jpg)

Map of Kowloon in 1915

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Hong_Kong_Kai_Tak_Airport_1971.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_Kai_Tak_Airport_1971.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Kai_Tak_Airport_1971.jpg)

Hong Kong's old airport, [Kai Tak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai_Tak_Airport "Kai Tak Airport"), was located in Kowloon Bay.

The part of Kowloon south of [Boundary Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boundary_Street "Boundary Street"), together with [Stonecutters Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stonecutters_Island "Stonecutters Island"), was ceded by [Qing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qing_empire "Qing empire") China to the United Kingdom under the [Convention of Peking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convention_of_Peking "Convention of Peking") of 1860. For many years the area remained largely undeveloped and was used by the British mainly for tiger-hunting expeditions.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[*[self-published source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The part of Kowloon north of Boundary Street ([New Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Kowloon "New Kowloon")) was leased by the British as part of the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories") under the 1898 [Second Convention of Peking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convention_between_the_United_Kingdom_and_China,_Respecting_an_Extension_of_Hong_Kong_Territory "Convention between the United Kingdom and China, Respecting an Extension of Hong Kong Territory") for 99 years. Within New Kowloon is Kowloon City, an [area of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_areas_of_Hong_Kong "List of areas of Hong Kong") where the [Kowloon Walled City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Walled_City "Kowloon Walled City") used to be located. The Kowloon Walled City itself was demolished in 1993. The same area was called Kwun Fu Cheung (官富場) during the [Song dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") (960–1279). "New Kowloon" has remained part of the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories").

Statutorily, "Kowloon" is only the area south of Boundary Street and Stonecutters Island, but in common use, New Kowloon is not regarded as part of the New Territories, but as an integral part of the Kowloon urban area whether north or south of Boundary Street.

Large-scale development of Kowloon began in the early 20th century, with the construction of the [Kowloon-Canton Railway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon-Canton_Railway_Corporation "Kowloon-Canton Railway Corporation") and the [Kowloon Wharf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Wharf "Kowloon Wharf"), but because of the close proximity of Kowloon's built-up area to [Kai Tak Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai_Tak_Airport "Kai Tak Airport"), building construction was limited by flight paths. As a result, compared to Hong Kong Island, Kowloon had a much lower skyline.[[1]](#cite_note-Fallon-1) After [World War II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Kowloon became extremely congested when [slums](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slum "Slum") for [refugees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") from the newly-established People's Republic of China gave way to [public housing estates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_housing_in_Hong_Kong "Public housing in Hong Kong"), mixed with private residential, commercial, and industrial areas.

The area of [reclaimed land](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_reclamation_in_Hong_Kong "Land reclamation in Hong Kong") that is now known as [West Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Kowloon "West Kowloon") was once home to a [dockyard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dockyard "Dockyard") for the [Royal Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").

The 1911 census recorded a population of 7,306, with most being [Hakka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hakka "Hakka").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The [invasion of China by Japan in 1937](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") caused the population of Kowloon to grow drastically. Between 1937 and 1939, 750,000 refugees arrived in Kowloon and nearby areas, with many not having residence.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit), 2,108,419 people lived in Kowloon.[[6]](#cite_note-hkcensus-6)

About 94.2% of Kowloon's residents are of [Han Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese")[*[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] ethnicity. The largest ethnic minority groups are [Indonesians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesians_in_Hong_Kong "Indonesians in Hong Kong") (1.8%), [Filipinos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filipinos_in_Hong_Kong "Filipinos in Hong Kong") (1.5%), [Indians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indians_in_Hong_Kong "Indians in Hong Kong") (0.5%), [Nepalese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nepalese_in_Hong_Kong "Nepalese in Hong Kong") (0.4%), and [British](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_people "British people") (0.3%).[[6]](#cite_note-hkcensus-6) Around 86% of Kowloon's residents use [Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_language "Cantonese language") as their usual language, while 2.3% use English and 1.2% use [Mandarin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese").[[6]](#cite_note-hkcensus-6)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Western_Harbour_Tunnel.JPG/250px-Western_Harbour_Tunnel.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Western_Harbour_Tunnel.JPG)

[Western Harbour Crossing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Harbour_Crossing "Western Harbour Crossing"), one of the tunnels that link Kowloon with Hong Kong Island

Kowloon is connected to Hong Kong Island by two road-only [tunnels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel") (the [Cross-Harbour Tunnel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-Harbour_Tunnel "Cross-Harbour Tunnel") and the [Western Harbour Crossing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Harbour_Crossing "Western Harbour Crossing")), three MTR railway tunnels ([Tsuen Wan line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tsuen_Wan_line "Tsuen Wan line"), [Tung Chung line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tung_Chung_line "Tung Chung line")/[Airport Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Airport_Express_(MTR) "Airport Express (MTR)") and [East Rail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shatin_to_Central_Link#Cross_Harbour_Section "Shatin to Central Link")) and one combined road and MTR rail link tunnel ([Eastern Harbour Tunnel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eastern_Harbour_Tunnel "Eastern Harbour Tunnel"), containing the [Tseung Kwan O line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tseung_Kwan_O_line "Tseung Kwan O line") and road traffic in separate parallel conduits). No bridges connect the island and Kowloon.

##### West Villa Restaurant (Causeway Bay)

West Villa Restaurant emphasises food hygiene, cleanliness of the dining environment and friendly service. Apart from traditional Cantonese cuisines, it constantly comes up with new dishes. Together with its hearty service, dining in West Villa is always a remarkable experience. Specialties include Braised Abalone with Oyster Sauce, Braised Fish Maw and Goose Web.

# The 9 Best Vantage Points for Viewing the Iconic Skyline

[Attractions](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/attractions) [First-Timer](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:persona/first-timer) [Harbour & Skyline](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/attractions/harbour-and-skyline) [Iconic Landmarks](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:core-experience/attractions/iconic-landmarks) [Photogenic](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/search.html?tag=dhkv2:purpose/photogenic)

There is no attraction in the city more iconic than the Hong Kong skyline. The deep waters between Hong Kong Island and the Kowloon Peninsula allowed a collection of fishing villages to evolve into an international centre of trade.

Today, the harbour remains the city’s beating heart and one of the world’s most stunning urban landscapes. With such a skyline, it deserves to be seen from multiple angles. Here are the best places to take in the view.

![Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront]()

Tsim Sha Tsui Waterfront

The most classic view of the harbour is from the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront. Marvel at the topological and architectural spectacle of Hong Kong, while admiring how the colourful lights from across the harbour dance on the waves. While you’re at the waterfront, head toward Tsim Sha Tsui East to check out the [Tsim Sha Tsui promenade](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-tsim-sha-tsui-promenade.html), [Avenue of Stars](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-avenue-of-stars.html), [Clock Tower](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-clock-tower.html), [Hong Kong Cultural Centre](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-hong-kong-cultural-centre.html) and enjoy the multimedia light show in the evening.

See More...

![The ‘Symphony of Lights’ multimedia light show puts Hong Kong’s iconic skyline front and centre.]()

‘A Symphony of Lights’

What could make the harbour even more stunning? The light and sound show ‘[A Symphony of Lights](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-a-symphony-of-lights.html)’! Every night at 8pm, iconic buildings from both sides of the harbour form a harmonious canvas for a sensational multimedia extravaganza, with a myriad of searchlights, lasers, LED screens and lighting working in symphony to put together a nightly spectacle that transforms the Hong Kong skyline into an outdoor audiovisual feast.

See More...

![The Star Ferry is a great way to explore Victoria Harbour and capture the skylines that line both sides.]()

Star Ferry

As one of the oldest forms of transportation in Hong Kong, the [Star Ferry](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-star-ferry-pier.html) has been faithfully carrying passengers across the harbour for more than a century. Although cross-harbour road and rail tunnels now connect both sides of the harbour, the Star Ferry still holds a dear place in locals’ hearts, providing a scenic way to cross the harbour that provides an up-close view of our iconic skyline, and an experience that is reminiscent of the charms of old Hong Kong.

See More...

![In Tsim Sha Tsui, the Ocean Deck Terminal offers a great view over Victoria Harbour, especially at sunset.]()

Ocean Terminal Deck

Located on the rooftop of Ocean Terminal’s five-storey extension building, which opened in 2017, [Ocean Terminal Deck](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-ocean-terminal-deck.html) is an observatory deck surrounded by the sea that offers a 270° panorama of the harbour as well as breathtaking views of Hong Kong Island and Kowloon. It is one of the best venues to enjoy Hong Kong’s sunset and evening views, while being close to the many shopping and dining options of [Harbour City](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-harbour-city.html).

See More...

![Victoria Peak is one of the most popular spots from which to enjoy views of the Hong Kong skyline.]()

The Peak

As the highest point on Hong Kong Island, [The Peak](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-the-peak.html) has long been Hong Kong’s most exclusive neighbourhood. But it’s the view that makes The Peak one of the most popular attractions in Hong Kong. By day, your eyes stretch across sparkling skyscrapers and the harbour all the way to the green hills of the New Territories. In the early evening, this panorama melts into pink and orange before reincarnating as a dazzling galaxy of light.

See More...

![Golden Bauhinia Square]()

Golden Bauhinia Square

For those who would like to experience the harbour with a taste of history, then the [Golden Bauhinia Square](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-golden-bauhinia-square.html) is a must visit. The bauhinia is the emblem of Hong Kong and the ‘Forever Blooming Bauhinia’ sculpture that sits on the promenade was a gift from the Central Government to mark the 1997 handover of Hong Kong. Every day from 7:50–8:03am (except the first day of each month) is a flag-raising ceremony performed by Hong Kong police officers.

See More...

![A ride on the Hong Kong Observation Wheel offers various vantage points over the skyline.]()

Hong Kong Observation Wheel

Located on the Central Harbourfront is Hong Kong’s own Ferris wheel – the [Hong Kong Observation Wheel](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-the-hong-kong-observation-wheel.html). At 60 metres high, it offers magnificent day and night-time views of the harbour. The wheel has 42 luxurious gondolas that can comfortably seat eight passengers. Be sure to keep an eye out for events, drinks and snacks on the surrounding plaza.

![Hong Kong water taxis are another way to experience the Hong Kong skyline.]()

Hong Kong Water Taxi

A harbour tour on the [Hong Kong Water Taxi](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-water-taxi.html)offers a unique way to experience the harbour. The daily route passes by the city’s most iconic attractions including Hong Kong Observation Wheel, International Financial Centre, the Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade and [West Kowloon Cultural District (WestK)](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-west-kowloon-cultural-district.html).

![Enjoy views of the Hong Kong skyline with drinks and food at the city’s many roofotp bars and restaurants.]()

Rooftop Bars

Hong Kong’s rooftop bars offer some jaw-dropping views of the city, with impressive food and drink to match. Sit down to a sophisticated evening of Japanese and Italian cuisines surrounded by floor-to-ceiling glass windows at Aqua. Sitting high above Nathan Road, the terrace at Eyebar is also a top contender for one of the best vantage points in the city.

For one of the best cocktail and wine lists in town, head to [Ozone](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-ozone.html) on the 118th floor of the ICC. As the highest bar in Hong Kong, and in the world, it offers a completely unobstructed view of western Hong Kong Island and Kowloon. On Hong Kong Island, a luxurious but relaxed fine dining experience showcasing the dazzling skyline awaits on the 49th floor of The Upper House at Salisterra.

See More...

Information on this page is subject to change without advance notice.
